EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface cold front crossed the region from west to east on January 29th. As it did so, a wave of low pressure developed along the frontal boundary over southwestern Virginia and moved northeastward to a position near Philadelphia by the evening of the 29th. This led to a rain and snow mix developing ahead of the cold front, but changing over to all snow following the frontal passage. In general, 1 to 2 inches of snow were observed across the far eastern West Virginia panhandle, with very little to no snow reported across the remainder of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were observed across Berkeley County, with 1.6 inches reported near Martinsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough and associated surface low tracked into the Ohio Valley late on the 19th. A large shield of warm advection precipitation moved over the area late on the 19th into the early 20th. Most locations received between one and two inches of precipitation, leading to isolated instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Opequon Creek at Martinsburg exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the indicated period, leading to flooding along portions of Douglas Grove Road (County Road 9/16) and Bowers Road. Additionally, water covered the stream access point at the Van Metre Ford Pedestrian Bridge. The peak level of 10.88 feet occurred at 6:15 PM on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air aloft overrunning colder surface air combined with lingering low level moisture to produce low clouds and widespread dense fog across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from around the area indicate visibility fell to a quarter of a mile or less in dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeastward through the Northeastern US during the early morning hours of January 9th. Strong northwest flow followed behind the system leading to the formation of upslope snow along and near the Allegheny Front from January 9th and into January 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were observed up to 8 inches across the western portions of Pendleton County, with the highest report near Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeastward through the Northeastern US during the early morning hours of January 9th. Strong northwest flow followed behind the system leading to the formation of upslope snow along and near the Allegheny Front from January 9th and into January 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches were observed across the western portions of Mineral County, with 7 inches being reported near Kitzmiller.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved through the central Appalachians on the 19th and 20th. Ahead of this system, rain fell through the day on the 19th. The highest rain amounts fell across the northern mountainous counties, where 1.25 to 1.75 inches fell. This lead to minor flooding along the Tygart Valley River in Randolph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tygart Valley River flooded near Mill Creek. The gage on the river at Mill Creek indicated the river rose out of its banks early on the 20th. It crested at around 11.6 feet, just above its 11 foot bankfull level, before sunrise that morning and returned to its banks just after noon. This closed a section of County Route 39.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the region on the morning of the 26th with showers preceding the boundary. There was a brief lull in activity behind the front, but as the upper disturbance crossed through in the afternoon an expansion in coverage resulted, some |storms also becoming severe.

A cold front, with a wave of low pressure lifting along it, moved across the central Appalachians on the 20th. This resulted in showers and thunderstorms. Some of these were strong to severe with damaging wind gusts and hail.

A very slow moving cold front sunk in from the north during the afternoon of the 26th. At the same time, a mid/upper level disturbance was crossing. The combination led to severe thunderstorms across the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a power line, knocking power out in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west in conjunction with a short wave trough aloft tapped into a fairly unstable air mass over Central Appalachia on the afternoon of the 17th. Storms fired up quickly that afternoon over West Virginia and continuing into the evening, with many impressive storms resulting in damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blew over onto power lines near Branchland and caused numerous power outages in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a very unstable atmosphere, thunderstorms developed in the middle Ohio River Valley on the afternoon of the 23rd. These storms quickly grew to severe levels, producing very large hail and sporadic wind damage as they moved across central West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many cars were dented by the hail, but no significant building damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving cold front sunk in from the north during the afternoon of the 26th. At the same time, a mid/upper level disturbance was crossing. The combination led to severe thunderstorms across the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power line were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure and remnants of a frontal boundary passed through the region late on the 4th and the early morning hours of the 5th. Showers streamed through from the southwest to the northeast along an approaching wave into an atmosphere with enhanced moisture. Saturated soils from the previous day's showers and storms combined with the showers and storms to produce flash flooding across portions of Virginia and West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported on Elk River Road near Tickelwash Avenue and at 5064 Elk River Road near Smith Avenue. A mudslide was also reported on the southbound lane of Elk River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable airmass was in place across the central Appalachians on the 2nd. Remnants of a decaying thunderstorms complex moved across the Ohio River that afternoon and were rejuvenated by the instability across western and central WV. Most of the storms remained below severe limits, but one storm created a very localized microburst in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined a microburst occurred in the Tornado community. Nearly 100 trees were snapped or uprooted. The Big Bend Golf course was near the epicenter of the microburst and was hit especially hard, with dozen of trees (mostly pine) blown down. Several outbuilding also suffered minor damage. The course remained close for over a month while the damage was cleaned up. The rain gutters were blown off the fire station in Tornado, and several houses nearby suffered minor roof damage. Power was out in the area for over a day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following several days of showers and thunderstorms, a final upper level disturbance crossed on the 31st bringing another round of showers and storms. A couple of the storms produced hail. The ground was wet enough that the additional 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rain that fell on the 31st caused flash flooding in the late morning and early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down near the intersection of Greenbrier Street and Millekin Drive, and another fell just up the road where Greenbrier turns into Indian Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving cold front sunk in from the north during the afternoon of the 26th. At the same time, a mid/upper level disturbance was crossing. The combination led to severe thunderstorms across the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a power line, knocking it down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple mesoscale convective systems within a hot and humid air mass brought lines of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  The storms produced hail up to the size of quarters and produced damaging winds that blew down numerous trees and power lines.  The trees also damaged several structures.  In addition, the winds destroyed bleachers, damaged a chain link fence, and blew down a light pole at a little league baseball field in Bellepoint Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell on Nubbins Ridge Road about two miles northwest of Spanishburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeastward through the Northeastern US during the early morning hours of January 9th. Strong northwest flow followed behind the system leading to the formation of upslope snow along and near the Allegheny Front from January 9th and into January 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches were observed across the western portions of Grant County, with 5.3 inches being reported near Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed across the south central United States during the daylight hours of January 12th as high pressure was positioned over the northeastern United States and southeastern Canada. The low pressure system then tracked into the southeastern United States during the overnight hours of January 12th and moved northeastward offshore near the Outer Banks of North Carolina through the day of January 13th. Widespread accumulating snow affected eastern West Virginia, with the highest totals near the I-81 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 7 inches were observed across western Mineral County, with 5.8 inches being reported near Keyser.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front approached the area during the day and into the evening of January 7th as a strong area of high pressure remained positioned off the Northeastern US coastline. Warm and moist air overrunning much cooler air at the surface led to areas of precipitation, some of which fell as light freezing rain across the terrain near the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of freezing rain were reported across western Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 19th, a low pressure system was located across the lower Mississippi Valley. This moved northeastward through the day and by the evening of the 19th was located across eastern Kentucky and Tennessee, intensifying to 994 mb as it did so. Overnight, the low tracked across Virginia and by mid morning on January 20th it was located near Philadelphia and had strengthened further to 988 mb. As it crossed the region, the system brought widespread mixed precipitation to eastern West Virginia, with light snow and ice accumulations being observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of ice were observed across western Pendleton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface cold front crossed the region from west to east on January 29th. As it did so, a wave of low pressure developed along the frontal boundary over southwestern Virginia and moved northeastward to a position near Philadelphia by the evening of the 29th. This led to a rain and snow mix developing ahead of the cold front, but changing over to all snow following the frontal passage. In general, 1 to 2 inches of snow were observed across the far eastern West Virginia panhandle, with very little to no snow reported across the remainder of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were observed across Jefferson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering low level moisture combined with light upslope flow and an approaching cold front to produce areas of light freezing drizzle along and near the Allegheny Front of West Virginia. Trace amounts to a light glaze of ice were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace to a light glaze of a few hundredths of an inch of ice were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air aloft overrunning colder surface air combined with lingering low level moisture to produce low clouds and widespread dense fog across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from around the area indicate visibility fell to a quarter of a mile or less in dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly intensified as it moved from the Mid-Mississippi Valley northward into the Great Lakes from late in the evening of February 24th and into the morning hours of February 25th. The low then tracked northeastward into Canada during the remainder of the 25th. A secondary area of low pressure also developed along the Mid-Atlantic coastline and moved into New England. Strong winds associated with the system led to high wind gusts across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 66 mph were measured, with the highest gust reported near Martinsburg. There were also reports of downed trees and power lines, as well as roof damage to two residences with tin roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure overhead of the region late on January 31st shifted eastward and offshore in the early morning of February 1st. A frontal boundary was positioned south of the region across southern Virginia, and a wave of low pressure developed along the boundary in the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 1st. This then moved eastward to the Virginia Tidewater by the evening hours. Warm air advection associated with this frontal wave led to widespread accumulating snow for the eastern West Virginia, with generally 2 to 4 inches across the lower elevations of the far eastern West Virginia panhandle, and 6 to 12 inches across the higher elevations near and along the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches were observed across Hampshire County, with a maximum of 6.0 inches reported near Romney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was in place over the northeastern United States during the overnight hours of February 19th. At the same time, low pressure was tracking northeastward from the south central US. Strong warm air advection and associated frontogenetical forcing led to a period of moderate to heavy snow that spread northeastward across eastern West Virginia on the morning of February 20th. As warmer air continued to move in aloft, precipitation then transitioned over to sleet and then freezing rain during the afternoon hours. By later that evening, the primary low was moving through the Great Lakes region as a secondary low moved along the Mid-Atlantic coastline. Eventually enough warm air moved into the region, changing all precipitation over to rain during the overnight hours on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 8 inches were observed across eastern Mineral County. Ice accumulations of up to two tenths of an inch were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the central Appalachains on the 8th, ushering in much colder air. Rain changed to snow overnight from the 8th into the 9th, with upslope snow continuing across the northern mountains of West Virginia into early on the 10th.   On the top of Snowshoe Mountain, the cooperative observer received 8 inches. According to the West Virginia Department of Highways, 6-8 inches of snow accumulated across other high ridges in Pocahontas and Randolph Counties.

Surface high pressure was located over the region on the morning of February 10th. This gave way to several waves of low pressure that moved near the region from the evening of the 10th and through the 12th. Several periods of mixed wintry precipitation affected eastern West Virginia during this time, with a light snow accumulation and ice accumulations of up to a quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were observed across Jefferson County. Ice accumulations of around 0.20 inches were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved from the southern Plains through the central Appalachians from the 11th through the 13th. This storm brought a mix of precipitation across the state of West Virginia, with mostly rain across the southwest, to freezing rain in the central mountainous counties, to snow and sleet across central and northern West Virginia. Along the eastern slopes of the central mountains, 0.2 to 0.3 of ice accumulated from late on the 12th through the early afternoon of the 13th. The automated surface observing station at the Raleigh County Memorial Airport measured 0.29 of ice.

A strong storm system moved from the southern Plains through the central Appalachians from the 11th through the 13th. This storm brought a mix of precipitation across the state of West Virginia, with mostly rain across the southwest, to freezing rain in the central mountainous counties, to snow and sleet across central and northern West Virginia. Along the eastern slopes of the central mountains, 0.2 to 0.3 of ice accumulated from late on the 12th through the early afternoon of the 13th. The automated surface observing station at the Raleigh County Memorial Airport measured 0.29 of ice.

Surface high pressure was in place over the northeastern United States during the overnight hours of February 19th. At the same time, low pressure was tracking northeastward from the south central US. Strong warm air advection and associated frontogenetical forcing led to a period of moderate to heavy snow that spread northeastward across eastern West Virginia on the morning of February 20th. As warmer air continued to move in aloft, precipitation then transitioned over to sleet and then freezing rain during the afternoon hours. By later that evening, the primary low was moving through the Great Lakes region as a secondary low moved along the Mid-Atlantic coastline. Eventually enough warm air moved into the region, changing all precipitation over to rain during the overnight hours on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches were observed across Berkeley County, with 6.3 inches reported near Johnsontown. Ice accumulations of up to one tenth of an inch were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was located over New England through the day on February 23rd. Overrunning warm and moist air advection associated with a developing system in the southern plains led to rain overspreading portions of eastern West Virginia. Along and near the Allegheny Front, temperatures at elevations between 1500 and 4000 feet were below freezing for much of the day and evening, which led to a glazing of ice up to about a tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of up to a tenth of an inch were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure rapidly intensified as it moved from the Mid-Mississippi Valley northward into the Great Lakes from late in the evening of February 24th and into the morning hours of February 25th. The low then tracked northeastward into Canada during the remainder of the 25th. A secondary area of low pressure also developed along the Mid-Atlantic coastline and moved into New England. Strong winds associated with the system led to high wind gusts across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 65 mph were measured, with the highest gust reported near Lost River. There were also reports of downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme cold arctic air surged into the northeast region January 30th. 500mb heights were close to 5040 meters, 850mb temperatures ranged from -26 to -30 degrees Celsius, and 925mb temperatures were near -22 to -26 degrees Celsius. With the quick drop in temperature in the boundary layer, a steep low level lapse rate was created near the surface. Due to this steep lapse rate, winds near 850mb to 925mb (averaging 20kts) were capable of reaching the surface and created wind chill temperatures of -22F and lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind chill of -30.0 F was measured at Canaan Heights on Jan 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surrounding areas had wind chill values below or at -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure was in place over the northeastern United States in the early morning hours of February 17th. At the same time, low pressure was tracking northeastward from the lower Mississippi Valley. By the afternoon the area of low pressure had progressed into the Ohio Valley with warm air advection occurring aloft over the Mid-Atlantic states, while cold surface temperatures remained. This led to areas of freezing rain and sleet during the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th for locations in eastern West Virginia. Trace amounts of ice were observed. Precipitation then changed to rain during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of ice were observed across western Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough carried cold air into the Northeast Region on January 21st. A 5200 meter height was recorded at 500mb, 850mb temperatures were -22C to -25C, and 925mb temperatures were -18C to -21C. The wind gust average throughout the area was 25mph while the ridges experienced higher gusts due to a tight pressure gradient from an exiting surface low pressure. The positioning of a building high pressure in the Midwest helped keep wind speeds west of the ridges fairly light.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind chill of -16.0 F was measured near Tunnelton, WV on the morning of Jan 21st. Surrounding areas got below -25 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more substantial system developed January 19-20 with low pressure developing over the Tennessee Valley and producing rainfall of 1 to 2 inches combined with modest snowmelt of up to 0.50 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Caldwell Tunnel on WV Highway 63 flooded and was about to be closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more substantial system developed January 19-20 with low pressure developing over the Tennessee Valley and producing rainfall of 1 to 2 inches combined with modest snowmelt of up to 0.50 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Alderson (ALDW2) measured 2.35 inches for the 24-hours period ending 700 AM local time on the 20th. This was the highest 24-hour amount in the month of January on record at this site. The old January daily record was 2.10 inches on January 31, 2013. Nearly continuous precipitation data extends back to 1945 at this station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more substantial system developed January 19-20 with low pressure developing over the Tennessee Valley and producing rainfall of 1 to 2 inches combined with modest snowmelt of up to 0.50 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water was reported flowing over Beagle Club Road at the intersection with WV Route 3 at Jumping Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wedge of cool high pressure was already in place across the mid-Atlantic states when deep low pressure entered the central Appalachians from the Tennessee Valley, bringing deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico northward along the Appalachian chain.  The low also brought with it a layer of warm air aloft where temperatures were above freezing, while surface temperatures held below freezing.  This resulted in a complex winter storm scenario where precipitation started off a snow, but transitioned to sleet and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a combination of mainly freezing rain and sleet.  Ice accumulations from freezing rain were around 0.25 inches thick across most of the county, while sleet accumulations were as much as 0.5 inches deep.

The low also brought with it a layer of warm air aloft where temperatures were above freezing, while surface temperatures held below freezing.  This resulted in a complex winter storm scenario where precipitation started off a snow, but transitioned to sleet and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a combination of freezing rain, sleet and snow to the county. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from 0.1 to 0.25 inches thick across most of the county, while sleet accumulations were as much as 1 inch.  Snow accumulations also were as much as 1 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sufficient upslope due to a shortwave trough crossing behind an exiting cold front helped scattered snow showers linger in the ridges. Cold temperatures in the lower levels were credited in keeping snow ratios higher. Low level lapse rates and good moisture in the dendritic growth zone was present at the time as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measurement of 7.0 inches of snow in a 24 hour time period was made 7 miles south of Davis at a ski resort. A snow measurement of 6.4 inches in Canaan Valley was made by a Co-op Observer. The Dept of Highways recorded 6.0 inches of snow in 24 hours at Thomas, WV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure overhead of the region late on January 31st shifted eastward and offshore in the early morning of February 1st. A frontal boundary was positioned south of the region across southern Virginia, and a wave of low pressure developed along the boundary in the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 1st. This then moved eastward to the Virginia Tidewater by the evening hours. Warm air advection associated with this frontal wave led to widespread accumulating snow for the eastern West Virginia, with generally 2 to 4 inches across the lower elevations of the far eastern West Virginia panhandle, and 6 to 12 inches across the higher elevations near and along the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were observed across western Mineral County, with a maximum of 9.0 inches reported near Keyser.

Low pressure tracked from the Plains to the upper Midwest on February 20th, before transitioning to a coastal system by the morning of the 21st. This low pushed a warm front northward across our region. Widespread precipitation was generated by deep moisture and an area of strong lift that accompanied the warm front. Precipitation started mainly as snow, but transitioned to a mostly brief period of mixed precipitation as warmer air moved in aloft. By late evening on the 20th, the passage of the warm front allowed all precipitation to change to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow was reported by the county WV DOH office at Glen Dale.

An arctic cold front crossed the region during the evening of March 5th, with very cold temperatures and strong northwest winds funneling behind the system from the night of the 5th and into the morning hours on the 6th. This was able to produce wind chill values as low as -10 degrees over the higher terrain near the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and strong winds produced wind chills as low as -12 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved from near North Carolina on the afternoon of March 1st to the nearshore waters of the Delmarva during the overnight hours, to near the benchmark by dawn on March 2nd. This resulted in widespread rain and trace amounts of ice being brought to eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of ice were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave moved through the Pittsburgh County Warning Area the evening of March 3rd/early morning of March 4th. Models from this event suggested that a coupled jet, saturation up through the dendritic growth zone, and frontogenesis across our area would support decent snowfall accumulations. While the Pittsburgh region accumulated just above three inches, the higher elevations got near 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of snow fell in the county in 12 hours. A measurement of 7.0 was taken by the public in Opekiska,WV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed over southeaster West Virginia. In the wake of the front,  high winds intensified in the overnight hours, particularly west of the Blue Ridge.  Impacts were amplified by waterlogged soil, allowing trees to be uprooted or displaced more easily.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site near Camp Creek, WV recorded a wind gust of 63 miles per hour.  In addition, a tree was reported blown down on College Avenue near Bluefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system organized over the southeastern United States on the morning of March 3rd and tracked northeastward to a position near coastal North Carolina by that evening. The low pressure then intensified overnight and moved to a position southeast of Cape Cod by the morning of March 4th. This system brought widespread precipitation to eastern West Virginia, with significant accumulations of snow falling in the higher elevations. Lesser amounts were observed in the lowlands of the eastern West Virginia panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were observed, with 5.0 inches measured near Johnsontown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure lifted from the central Plains on the morning of the 23rd into the western Great Lakes by the 24th.  A cold front then rushed across the region during the late morning/early afternoon hours of the 24th.  Some rain showers accompanied the front, but the main story was the prolonged period of damaging wind gusts that followed the frontal passage. A very tight pressure gradient, 3 hour pressure rises on the order of 7-8 mb/hr, and deep mixing of up to 700 mb allowed for the efficient downward transport of strong wind aloft. The result was an extended period of 40-60 MPH wind gusts across the region.  Several reports of peak gusts of 60 MPH or greater were received, ||The highest recorded gust was 74 MPH at a mesonet sensor east of Canaan Heights in Tucker County, WV.  A number of peak wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH were observed as well. There were widespread reports of downed trees, power lines, and structural damage across the entire region, with some examples noted in the county entries. Thankfully, no injuries were reported from wind effects.||Power outages were widespread as well.  A peak of 91,000 outages were recorded in West Virginia on the night of the 24th.  Full restoration took multiple days due to the extent of damage and the initially unsafe working conditions due to the ongoing wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county 911 call center stated that there were nearly 40 reports of downed trees across Hancock County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed along the North Carolina coast and tracked northward toward the Delmarva on the 21st. Heavy rainfall spread northward in advance of the low and then wrapped around the backside of the low as it tracked further north. Prolonged moderate to heavy rainfall resulted in one to three inches of rain across portions of West Virginia, leading to isolated instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Opequon Creek at Martinsburg exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Water covered backyards of homes on Bowers Road (County Road 1/1), as well as the stream access point at the Van Metre Ford Pedestrian Bridge. Douglas Grove Road (County Road 9/16) was flooded and impassible. Water also approached Golf Course Road (County Road 36) and Grapevine Road (County Road 9/14). The peak level of 12.07 feet occurred at 2:15 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low moved eastward from Missouri and Arkansas and into Tennessee through the daytime hours of April 19th, and then moved northward into Kentucky during the night. At the surface, low pressure moved from near Tennessee in the morning to eastern Kentucky by the evening, and into Ohio by late in the night, intensifying along its track. This brought strong forcing across the Mid-Atlantic and southeastern United States with divergent flow aloft and deep moist southerly flow at the surface. Moderate instability developed by the afternoon hours with strong speed and directional shear aloft. This led to two distinct rounds of showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern West Virginia, with the first round in the mid to late afternoon hours, and a second round in the evening. Some of the thunderstorms in the mid to late afternoon became severe with locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported blown down or snapped near South Mill Creek Dam in the 1100 block of South Mill Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low moved eastward from Missouri and Arkansas and into Tennessee through the daytime hours of April 19th, and then moved northward into Kentucky during the night. At the surface, low pressure moved from near Tennessee in the morning to eastern Kentucky by the evening, and into Ohio by late in the night, intensifying along its track. This brought strong forcing across the Mid-Atlantic and southeastern United States with divergent flow aloft and deep moist southerly flow at the surface. Moderate instability developed by the afternoon hours with strong speed and directional shear aloft. This led to two distinct rounds of showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern West Virginia, with the first round in the mid to late afternoon hours, and a second round in the evening. Some of the thunderstorms in the mid to late afternoon became severe with locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Trough Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted through the central Appalachains on the 11th and 12th, bringing a prolonged period of rain. The heaviest rain fell in Harrison County, where 1-2 inches were measured. This led to flooding by the morning of the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and humidity through the day on June 29th led to a moderately unstable air mass by the afternoon. A weak pressure trough developed and heights began to fall by the evening as an upper level ridge broke down. Also late in the evening, a cold front began to approach, increasing forcing across the region. Several rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms developed from the mid afternoon hours through well past sunset. Some thunderstorms became strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in the Shepherdstown area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge that was in place began to break down during the afternoon hours of July 21st as a cold front moved through the Midwest and an upper-level trough dug into the northern Plains. This allowed additional moisture into the area and when|combined with a weakening capping inversion from height falls it was enough for scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop. With substantial instability, some of these thunderstorms became strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down across WV-220/2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front moved slowly through the Ohio Valley on the afternoon of July 31st, with a secondary surface trough positioned just east of the I-95 corridor. Aloft, an upper level trough slowly moved across the region. As instability increased during the afternoon hours, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed, and one became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters (1.0 in diameter) was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms trained over the same area as they developed along a cold front just to the north. The training convection led to heavy amounts of rain in a short period of time for areas near the Appalachian Mountains in West Virginia. The heavy amounts of rain caused creeks and streams to rapidly rise out of their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue was underway for a home surrounded by water near the Gas and Go.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Vertically-stacked low pressure drifted northward across the upper Ohio Valley on April 20th.  The cold pool associated with the upper low aided in the generation of thunderstorms during the afternoon.  A few reports of hail were received, a couple of which reached an inch in diameter.  Due to training thunderstorms, some pockets of heavy rain were observed as well, but no reports of flooding were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Falling heights, an increasing southwest flow aloft, an approaching cold front, and strengthening pressure trough overhead combined with instability from a hot and humid air mass in place to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours of July 22nd. With high levels of CAPE and increased shear profiles, thunderstorms became strong to severe as they organized into linear structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8-inch diameter tree was snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure emerged from the Lower Mississippi Valley and rode up the Ohio Valley on April 14th. Destablization was able to occur as a dry slot formed behind morning rain, with CAPE building up to around 1500 J/kg.  Also, 70-80 knots of effective bulk shear was able to organize convective updrafts and allow for some storm rotation.  Convection quickly organized into a linear structure ahead of a cold front as it crossed southeast Ohio during the late afternoon, reaching the PA/OH border around 7:00 PM and reaching the Laurel Highlands by 10:00 PM.||For this event, the tornado risk was perceived to be relatively high, with the Storm Prediction Center forecasting numeric probabilities in the vicinity of the Ohio River that were of an unusually high level.  No tornado activity was confirmed in West Virginia from this event, with only a few severe weather reports received overall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a morning of sunshine on the 3rd, an upper level disturbance crossed the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians during the afternoon. The combination of hot and humid conditions and the disturbance aloft resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail caused damage to siding.

A stationary front was positioned across the eastern West Virginia panhandle southeastward through southern and central Virginia through the day on May 3rd. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and south of this boundary and across the terrain during the afternoon and evening hours. Enough instability and wind shear was present for one of these thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail (1.0 inch in diameter) was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air aloft overrunning cooler surface air combined with lingering low level moisture to produce low clouds and widespread dense fog across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from around the area indicated that visibility was reduced to a quarter mile or less in dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west in conjunction with a short wave trough aloft tapped into a fairly unstable air mass over Central Appalachia on the afternoon of the 17th. Storms fired up quickly that afternoon over West Virginia and continuing into the evening, with many impressive storms resulting in damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in the St. Albans area. One tree in particular fell onto a house and caused notable damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was located over the region on the morning of February 10th. This gave way to several waves of low pressure that moved near the region from the evening of the 10th and through the 12th. Several periods of mixed wintry precipitation affected eastern West Virginia during this time, with a light snow accumulation and ice accumulations of up to a quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of up to 1 inch were observed across eastern Grant County, along with ice accumulations of around 0.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into West Virginia on the evening of the 23rd, promoting widespread showers and a few isolated thunderstorms due to the close proximity of an approaching cold front. Generally, 1 to 1.75 inches of rain fell between the evening of the 23rd and the morning of the 24th. This led to flooding across southern and western portions of the state.||Behind the cold front, very gusty winds developed with most areas seeing gusts of 40 to 45 knots. Stronger gusts were measured across the higher terrain of the northern mountainous counties. The ASOS at Elkins measured a 51 knot gust, and on the top of Snowshoe mountain, gusts of 60 to 75 knots were measured. Combined with the soggy ground, these winds led to widespread power outages due to downed trees and power lines. Tens of thousands of power customers lost power, and it took a couple days for all service to be restored.||In addition to the downed trees, more noteworthy damage occurred in Huntington, where some windows were broken in the Prichard Building, which stands 13 stories tall. Glass fell onto the road below, which was closed while crew cleaned up the glass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads around the town of Hamlin became restricted or closed due to high water along the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong center of high pressure over the northeastern United States wedged cold air along the east side of the Appalachian Mountains. Low pressure in the Gulf brought moisture into the region as it tracked northeast over the Carolinas and Virginia. The enhanced moisture combined with the dammed cold air created a complex winter storm that produced snow, sleet and freezing rain in southeastern West Virginia, with the upper elevations experiencing the highest impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced freezing rain and snow in Mercer county. Ice from freezing rain reached a thickness of 0.15 inches near Athens, while snow amounts ranged from 0.1 to a maximum of 4.0 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced freezing rain, sleet and snow in Summers county. Ice from freezing rain was reported in trace amounts, and sleet accumulated to a depth of 0.5 inches near Hinton, while snow amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches around the county.

An area of low pressure rapidly intensified as it moved from the Mid-Mississippi Valley northward into the Great Lakes from late in the evening of February 24th and into the morning hours of February 25th. The low then tracked northeastward into Canada during the remainder of the 25th. A secondary area of low pressure also developed along the Mid-Atlantic coastline and moved into New England. Strong winds associated with the system led to high wind gusts across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 58 mph were estimated. There were also reports of downed trees and power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 75 mph were measured, with the highest gust reported near Streby. There were also reports of downed trees and power lines.

Strong winds associated with the system led to high wind gusts across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 60 mph were measured, with the highest gust reported near Brushy Run. There were also reports of downed trees and power lines.

The low then tracked northeastward into Canada during the remainder of the 25th. A secondary area of low pressure also developed along the Mid-Atlantic coastline and moved into New England. Strong winds associated with the system led to high wind gusts across eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 58 mph were estimated. There were also reports of downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into West Virginia on the evening of the 23rd, promoting widespread showers and a few isolated thunderstorms due to the close proximity of an approaching cold front. Generally, 1 to 1.75 inches of rain fell between the evening of the 23rd and the morning of the 24th. This led to flooding across southern and western portions of the state.||Behind the cold front, very gusty winds developed with most areas seeing gusts of 40 to 45 knots. Stronger gusts were measured across the higher terrain of the northern mountainous counties. The ASOS at Elkins measured a 51 knot gust, and on the top of Snowshoe mountain, gusts of 60 to 75 knots were measured. Combined with the soggy ground, these winds led to widespread power outages due to downed trees and power lines. Tens of thousands of power customers lost power, and it took a couple days for all service to be restored.||In addition to the downed trees, more noteworthy damage occurred in Huntington, where some windows were broken in the Prichard Building, which stands 13 stories tall. Glass fell onto the road below, which was closed while crew cleaned up the glass.

Low pressure tracked across Ohio and Pennsylvania on February 6th, pushing a warm front northward which stalled over Pennsylvania.  A second low pressure center tracked along this boundary, strengthening as it tracked into Michigan and Ontario.  Abundant moisture combined with isentropic lift to provide two main periods of rainfall, with another round of showers accompanying a cold front on the night of February 7th.  ||Rainfall totals from 7 AM on the 6th to 7 AM on the 8th totaled one to three inches across the area.  The area of 2 inch or greater rainfall extended from Latrobe PA southwest through Uniontown and Waynesburg, and then across portions of the northern West Virginia panhandle south of Wheeling into portions of southeast Ohio.  Flooding reports mainly consisted of overflowing streams and road flooding, although a couple of water rescues were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County 911 officials reported minor creek flooding along highway 180.

High pressure overhead of the region late on January 31st shifted eastward and offshore in the early morning of February 1st. A frontal boundary was positioned south of the region across southern Virginia, and a wave of low pressure developed along the boundary in the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 1st. This then moved eastward to the Virginia Tidewater by the evening hours. Warm air advection associated with this frontal wave led to widespread accumulating snow for the eastern West Virginia, with generally 2 to 4 inches across the lower elevations of the far eastern West Virginia panhandle, and 6 to 12 inches across the higher elevations near and along the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches were observed across eastern Mineral County, with 6.6 inches measured near Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated soils from the previous evening's rain, light winds, and clearing skies led to the formation of fog, some of which became dense with visibilities reduced to a quarter of a mile or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations indicated that visibilities were reduced to a quarter of a mile or less in dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface warm front and quick moving mid level disturbance brought snow to the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians on the 1st. Arctic air was in place ahead of this system, leading to very efficient snowfall which quickly accumulated on the ground. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain by the afternoon as warmer air moved in aloft. Along the Ohio River in Tyler County, 5 inches of snow fell near Sistersville. Similar amounts were measured across northern Doddridge County, with 5 inches in Center Point and 4 inches in West Union. In Harrison County, 5-6 inches were common around and north of Clarksburg. Six inches of snow also fell across much of Taylor County, and into the Elkins area of Randolph County. Harmon, in northeast Randolph County received 8 inches of snow.

Low pressure tracked from the Plains to the upper Midwest on February 20th, before transitioning to a coastal system by the morning of the 21st. This low pushed a warm front northward across our region. Widespread precipitation was generated by deep moisture and an area of strong lift that accompanied the warm front. Precipitation started mainly as snow, but transitioned to a mostly brief period of mixed precipitation as warmer air moved in aloft. By late evening on the 20th, the passage of the warm front allowed all precipitation to change to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both a co-op observer and the public reported 5 inches at Davis.

From February 20th to 24th, multiple rounds of precipitation passed across southeast West Virginia resulting in liquid accumulations ranging from around 1.5 to 3 inches. The storm started out on the 20th as a combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the mountains with liquid equivalents of 0.50 up to 2 inches. The next storm arrived early late on February 21st, with warmer air arriving with the system allowing the precipitation to fall mainly as rain, which helped to quickly melt the frozen precipitation which fell during the 24-36 hours prior. With soils already saturated from previous precipitation, runoff from this storm caused mainly minor stream flooding and at least one significant landslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads along the Bluestone River in Montcalm were flooded with high water signs placed in several locations. The Bluestone River gage at Spanishburg (SPNW2) crested at 13.10 feet, slightly above the Minor Flood Stage of 12 feet. Well downstream at Pipestem (PIPW2) the Bluestone crested at 11.84 feet early on the 24th, above the Minor flood stage of 10 feet.

Several waves of low pressure at the surface moved up the Ohio River Valley on the 6th and 7th. Rainfall amounts in western West Virginia through the period totaled around 2 inches. This led to several areas of minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along Little Pond Run closed a section of College Parkway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fishpot Road was closed due to flooding on Fishpot Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into West Virginia on the evening of the 23rd, promoting widespread showers and a few isolated thunderstorms due to the close proximity of an approaching cold front. Generally, 1 to 1.75 inches of rain fell between the evening of the 23rd and the morning of the 24th. This led to flooding across southern and western portions of the state.||Behind the cold front, very gusty winds developed with most areas seeing gusts of 40 to 45 knots. Stronger gusts were measured across the higher terrain of the northern mountainous counties. The ASOS at Elkins measured a 51 knot gust, and on the top of Snowshoe mountain, gusts of 60 to 75 knots were measured. Combined with the soggy ground, these winds led to widespread power outages due to downed trees and power lines. Tens of thousands of power customers lost power, and it took a couple days for all service to be restored.||In addition to the downed trees, more noteworthy damage occurred in Huntington, where some windows were broken in the Prichard Building, which stands 13 stories tall. Glass fell onto the road below, which was closed while crew cleaned up the glass.

Low pressure tracked from the Plains to the upper Midwest on February 20th, before transitioning to a coastal system by the morning of the 21st. This low pushed a warm front northward across our region. Widespread precipitation was generated by deep moisture and an area of strong lift that accompanied the warm front. Precipitation started mainly as snow, but transitioned to a mostly brief period of mixed precipitation as warmer air moved in aloft. By late evening on the 20th, the passage of the warm front allowed all precipitation to change to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was reported by the county DOH office near Wellsburg, and 1.5 inches was noted by the public at Follansbee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From February 20th to 24th, multiple rounds of precipitation passed across southeast West Virginia resulting in liquid accumulations ranging from around 1.5 to 3 inches. The storm started out on the 20th as a combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain across the mountains with liquid equivalents of 0.50 up to 2 inches. The next storm arrived early late on February 21st, with warmer air arriving with the system allowing the precipitation to fall mainly as rain, which helped to quickly melt the frozen precipitation which fell during the 24-36 hours prior. With soils already saturated from previous precipitation, runoff from this storm caused mainly minor stream flooding and at least one significant landslide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Princeton (PRIW2) measured 1.12 inches for the 24-hour period ending at 700 AM local time on February 23rd, which is the highest ever for the date. The old record was 0.85 inches in 1987. Nearly complete precipitation data for this station exists from 1901-1914 and from 1941 up until the present.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several news sources reported a large landslide along Route 112 near Oakvale. Photos from the scene showed a home had been moved downhill and the road was blocked by a large mass of debris. The area had received several days of rainfall ranging from 1 to 2.5 inches which was preceded by snow and mixed precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved out of the Plains into the Great Lakes on the 19th and 20th. Cold air was in place along the eastern slopes of the central Appalachians as precipitation started late on the 19th, with snow turning to freezing rain as low level warm air moved in. Total ice accumulations of 0.2 to 0.3 were seen across portions of eastern Fayette and Raleigh Counties by mid morning on the 20th. Farther north along the ridges of Pocahontas and Randolph Counties, more sleet and snow fell. Still, a period of freezing rain resulted in enough ice accretion on several of the ski lifts at Snowshoe to necessitate taking them out of service for a brief time on the 20th. Warmer air continued to overtake the region through the day, with periods of moderate to heavy rainfall into the afternoon. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 1.5 inches. This rain fell on already saturated soil and led to minor flooding in multiple areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into West Virginia on the evening of the 23rd, promoting widespread showers and a few isolated thunderstorms due to the close proximity of an approaching cold front. Generally, 1 to 1.75 inches of rain fell between the evening of the 23rd and the morning of the 24th. This led to flooding across southern and western portions of the state.||Behind the cold front, very gusty winds developed with most areas seeing gusts of 40 to 45 knots. Stronger gusts were measured across the higher terrain of the northern mountainous counties. The ASOS at Elkins measured a 51 knot gust, and on the top of Snowshoe mountain, gusts of 60 to 75 knots were measured. Combined with the soggy ground, these winds led to widespread power outages due to downed trees and power lines. Tens of thousands of power customers lost power, and it took a couple days for all service to be restored.||In addition to the downed trees, more noteworthy damage occurred in Huntington, where some windows were broken in the Prichard Building, which stands 13 stories tall. Glass fell onto the road below, which was closed while crew cleaned up the glass.

Steady rain on February 24th caused rises on many streams.  One stream, Patterson Creek, was already running high from prior rain and exceeded flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Patterson Creek exceeded the 9 foot flood stage during the indicated period. The peak level of 9.28 feet occurred at 5:45pm EST on February 24th.  Flooding occurred on Foreback Road and Georges Run Road.

A ridge of high pressure was in place over the northeastern United States in the early morning hours of February 17th. At the same time, low pressure was tracking northeastward from the lower Mississippi Valley. By the afternoon the area of low pressure had progressed into the Ohio Valley with warm air advection occurring aloft over the Mid-Atlantic states, while cold surface temperatures remained. This led to areas of freezing rain and sleet during the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th for locations in eastern West Virginia. Trace amounts of ice were observed. Precipitation then changed to rain during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of ice were observed across Jefferson County.

CZ_NAME: RIDGES OF E MONONGALIA AND NW PRESTON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure tracked across the middle Ohio Valley along a nearly stationary boundary, a fast-moving upper level shortwave provided the support for widespread snowfall. Most of the higher snow totals of 4 inches or more were recorded south of Interstate 70. The higher terrain of West Virginia and Maryland received better dynamic support from a crossing jet streak, providing better lift in a moist dendritic growth zone. Resulting snow-to-liquid ratios of 20:1 to 25:1 helped to create snow totals reaching double digits in a few cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 6 to 7 inches of snow fell across this zone, based on trained spotter reports of 6 inches at Reedsville and 7 inches at Cheat Lake.

Surface high pressure was located over the region on the morning of February 10th. This gave way to several waves of low pressure that moved near the region from the evening of the 10th and through the 12th. Several periods of mixed wintry precipitation affected eastern West Virginia during this time, with a light snow accumulation and ice accumulations of up to a quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of up to 1 inch were observed across western Grant County, along with ice accumulations of around 0.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure tracked across the middle Ohio Valley along a nearly stationary boundary, a fast-moving upper level shortwave provided the support for widespread snowfall. Most of the higher snow totals of 4 inches or more were recorded south of Interstate 70. The higher terrain of West Virginia and Maryland received better dynamic support from a crossing jet streak, providing better lift in a moist dendritic growth zone. Resulting snow-to-liquid ratios of 20:1 to 25:1 helped to create snow totals reaching double digits in a few cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of 5 inches of snow were received for this event from a social media user in Paden City, a trained spotter in Reader, and the county emergency manager in New Martinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved out of the Plains into the Great Lakes on the 19th and 20th. Cold air was in place along the eastern slopes of the central Appalachians as precipitation started late on the 19th, with snow turning to freezing rain as low level warm air moved in. Total ice accumulations of 0.2 to 0.3 were seen across portions of eastern Fayette and Raleigh Counties by mid morning on the 20th. Farther north along the ridges of Pocahontas and Randolph Counties, more sleet and snow fell. Still, a period of freezing rain resulted in enough ice accretion on several of the ski lifts at Snowshoe to necessitate taking them out of service for a brief time on the 20th. Warmer air continued to overtake the region through the day, with periods of moderate to heavy rainfall into the afternoon. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 1.5 inches. This rain fell on already saturated soil and led to minor flooding in multiple areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middle Island Creak near Little flooded, closing a section of Stewarts Run Road and flooding low lying fields along the creek. The stream gage near Little showed the creek rose above its 14 foot bankfull level just after midnight on the 21st. It crested at 15 feet around sunrise, and returned to its banks by late morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into West Virginia on the evening of the 23rd, promoting widespread showers and a few isolated thunderstorms due to the close proximity of an approaching cold front. Generally, 1 to 1.75 inches of rain fell between the evening of the 23rd and the morning of the 24th. This led to flooding across southern and western portions of the state.||Behind the cold front, very gusty winds developed with most areas seeing gusts of 40 to 45 knots. Stronger gusts were measured across the higher terrain of the northern mountainous counties. The ASOS at Elkins measured a 51 knot gust, and on the top of Snowshoe mountain, gusts of 60 to 75 knots were measured. Combined with the soggy ground, these winds led to widespread power outages due to downed trees and power lines. Tens of thousands of power customers lost power, and it took a couple days for all service to be restored.||In addition to the downed trees, more noteworthy damage occurred in Huntington, where some windows were broken in the Prichard Building, which stands 13 stories tall. Glass fell onto the road below, which was closed while crew cleaned up the glass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local residents of Tornado noticed a car submerged on Ferrell Road due to Smith Creek coming out of its banks. Emergency responders determined the driver was able to escape on their own.  High water was reported in many areas across Kanawha County on the 24th. This included parts of Riverside Drive in South Charleston, Left Fork Leatherwood Road in Clendenin, and Capehart Lane in Institute. High water also closed portions of Coal River Road near Tornado as well as Rutledge Road in North Charleston, where debris covered the roadways even after the flood waters receded. Other areas where high water on roads occurred were Spruce Street and Monroe Avenue in St. Albans, 901 West Dupont Avenue near Belle, Big Chimney Boulevard and Pennsylvania Avenue in Big Chimney, and Elk River Road South in Elkview.

An upper level trough moved southeastward from the Great Lakes and into the Mid-Atlantic states during the day of August 15th. At the surface, a boundary also moved southeastward from Ohio and into the region. With building instability, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the higher elevations in the mid afternoon hours, and then moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening. With relatively strong westerly to west-northwesterly flow aloft, deep layer shear increased and that, combined with the instability, led to some thunderstorms becoming strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto WV-28 outside of Ridgeley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept across the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians on the 2nd. Strong to severe thunderstorms moved through along and ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourpole Creek flooded, causing high water on Hal Greer Boulevard near I-64. The intersection of 5th Avenue and 25th Street also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow out ahead of a frontal boundary ushered in a hot, humid, and therefore unstable air mass into the region during the afternoon and evening hours of August 6th. A weak surface trough along with terrain circulations caused isolated showers and|thunderstorms to develop across portions of the region. One thunderstorm was able to become locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Warm Springs Road near the intersection with Shenandoah Junction Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All four counties in southeast West Virginia covered by the NWS office in Blacksburg entered the severe drought classification  (D2 on  a scale from D0 to D4) on the October 1st issuance of the weekly U.S. Drought Monitor and remained there for the next three weeks (until issuance of October 22nd). Dry conditions actually began as far back as July and August in some areas but the rainfall was very uneven. It was a very dry and hot September that really caused a rapid deterioration in the drought situation in much of West Virginia. Several NWS COOP sites had all-time driest Septembers or even the driest of any month. The dry conditions were aggravated by an early October heat wave with numerous record or near-record highs occurred from October 1st through 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) was depicted across nearly all of Monroe County on the U.S. Drought Monitor from October 1st to October 22nd. An extremely dry and very warm September led to the worsening conditions in the county. The COOP site at Greenville 1 SE (GRVW2) had only 0.53 inches of rain in September. Agricultural damages were severe, with estimated losses per the USDA Farm Service Agency at $920,000 from pastures and $1.4 million from hay losses.

The NWS COOP site at Bluefield (BLFW2) in Mercer County recorded a mere 0.26 inches of rain in September. This was the driest of any month on record at this station with nearly complete data back to 1999. The previous record was 0.30 inches in October, 2000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide occurred due to heavy rainfall along Route 2 between the towns of Paden City and Sistersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system and strong cold front crossed through West Virginia on the evening of the 24th, passing through a very warm and unstable environment. A line of severe thunderstorms stretched across Central Appalachia that afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage as well as two tornadoes touching down near the city of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were brought down near the town of Wallace. Some of the power lines fell onto a car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 4 miles east northeast of Alum Creek, just inside the Kanawha County line. The tornado continued moving northeastward in the vicinity of Route 119 and eventually dissipated near the Kanawha River as it approached downtown Charleston. The tornado was not continuously on the ground, frequently touching down along its path. Many trees were snapped and/or uprooted, in addition to minor roof damage. Damage also occurred at Berry Hills Country Club. The survey team also noticed several areas of non-tornadic wind damage in close proximity to the tornado path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system and strong cold front crossed through West Virginia on the evening of the 24th, passing through a very warm and unstable environment. A line of severe thunderstorms stretched across Central Appalachia that afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage as well as two tornadoes touching down near the city of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The field goal posts at Laidley Field were bent, and a section of the artificial turf was ripped and rippled. Construction was ongoing at Laidley, and the ends of the turf were unsecured as the track was replaced. This allowed the wind to get under the turf and lift it slightly off the ground, resulting in the damage. Several trees were also blown over along Kanawha Boulevard near the Capitol and in Ruffner Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system and strong cold front crossed through West Virginia on the evening of the 24th, passing through a very warm and unstable environment. A line of severe thunderstorms stretched across Central Appalachia that afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage as well as two tornadoes touching down near the city of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just north of Spring Hill Cemetery and traveled north northwest towards the eastern portion of Twilight Drive, tearing down several trees on the highest elevation peak. The tornado then traveled down the northern side of the hill and moved across an open field. Part of a roof was blown off an office building and a batch of large healthy trees were blown down in a circular motion at the end of the field. This is where the tornado is estimated to have been at its peak strength. The tornado then continued north northwest where it blew a steeple off of a church along Keystone Drive and then continued up the adjacent hill on the southern edge of the Charleston Airport, where it then dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal boundary set up across Ohio and Pennsylvania on the 17th, and wavered about the region until it was kicked off to the east by a stronger frontal boundary late on the 20th.  Flooding and flash flooding remained a concern through the period given an ideal setup for training storms, as well as efficient rainfall production associated with observed high PWATs and deeper warm cloud depths.   Flooding issues were mainly confined to the northern panhandle of West Virginia, with reports of road closures and a couple instances of trees felled by the floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County 911 dispatchers reported that multiple roads were flooded across the northern part of Hancock County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed southward into West Virginia and stalled nearby during the early morning hours on the 30th. Thunderstorms progressing through the area resulted in one to two inches of rainfall, leading to flash flooding across northeast West Virginia.  In Randolph County, just over three inches of rainfall fell in a three hour time span, leading to multiple mudslides, washed out roads, and water rescues. An automatic gauge on Haines Knob near Job measured 3.1 inches. The President of the United States signed a Disaster Declaration for the Harman area of Randolph County due to the significant amount of flooding that occurred with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapidly rising waters impacted a campground in Dryfork, where a truck was swept away. Campers were stranded and were unable to evacuate due to all roads out of the campground being blocked by multiple mudslides. This occurred in an area with no cell service.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported by the county emergency manager near the intersection of 16th and Main Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed southward into West Virginia and stalled nearby during the early morning hours on the 30th. Thunderstorms progressing through the area resulted in one to two inches of rainfall, leading to flash flooding across northeast West Virginia.  In Randolph County, just over three inches of rainfall fell in a three hour time span, leading to multiple mudslides, washed out roads, and water rescues. An automatic gauge on Haines Knob near Job measured 3.1 inches. The President of the United States signed a Disaster Declaration for the Harman area of Randolph County due to the significant amount of flooding that occurred with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding led to several roads being closed overnight in the Harman area, causing infrastructure damage throughout the town. The local water and sewage plant were significantly impacted and knocked offline. Numerous roads and culverts were washed out and several homes and trailers were pushed off their foundations. In total, 13 homes were damaged. Three of them were rendered uninhabitable. Sewer lines in Harman were also washed out. Many water rescues were conducted. Across the Monongahela National Forest, 68 miles of train track were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment, plus residual outflow boundaries from previous convection, helped to expand thunderstorm coverage during the evening of the 29th ahead of a sagging cold front.   Storms pushed across the northern panhandle of West Virginia between 7 PM and midnight.  Strong winds accompanying these storms produced a few instances of wind damage in Brooke County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported to be blown down on McAdoo Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment, plus residual outflow boundaries from previous convection, helped to expand thunderstorm coverage during the evening of the 29th and into the early morning hours of the 30th ahead of a sagging cold front.   Storms pushed across Preston and Tucker Counties starting after 10 PM on the 29th. Periods of moderate to heavy rain occurred until around 5 AM the following morning as backbuilding storms continued through the night. Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were observed over southern portions of Preston County and over much of Tucker County, resulting in some reports of flash flooding.  A few water rescues were necessary at Arnold Park Campground, as well as in Jenningston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local fire department reported that State Route 24 was closed due to flooding in multiple locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving frontal boundary dropped south from the Great Lakes during the first two days of June.  On the 2nd, as the front sank across Ohio and Pennsylvania, storms were able to fire near and south of the Mason-Dixon Line around midday, with activity continuing through the afternoon.  Surface-based CAPE values near and over 2000 J/kg were observed.  While the day before was characterized more by damaging wind gusts, sufficient shear and CAPE in the -10C to -30C layer meant that large hail reports ruled the day.  Quarter to ping-pong ball sized hail was observed in a few cases.

A strong mid-level trough swung across the Plains and the Great Lakes on the 31st.  The trough took on a negative tilt as it crossed the middle and upper Ohio Valley during the evening.  Deepening surface low pressure rode northward into the eastern Great Lakes as well, which pushed a cold front across the region during the evening.  An unseasonably strong feed of moisture developed ahead of this system, triggering heavy rainfall.  1 to 2 inches of rain fell across southwest Pennsylvania, with isolated higher totals of up to 2.5 inches.  This led to several reports of road and small-stream flooding in the region.||In addition, the negatively tilted trough, strong pressure gradient, and mixing provided by the cold front led to a period of high winds across the region.   Many gusts of 45 to 50 MPH were observed, with a high gust of 64 MPH reported in eastern Tucker County, WV.  Many reports of tree and power line damage were received, and power outages numbered into the thousands of customers.  Wind impacts were likely more widespread than reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down near Elm Street and Miller Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the region during the day of June 2nd, and that combined with broadly falling heights and increasing instability and wind shear, led to the development of scattered strong to locally severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of dimes (0.75 in diameter) was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold over the Ohio River Valley invited copious amounts of moisture into the region. Coincident with afternoon heating, this led to a few severe thunderstorms in West Virginia, resulting in large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house in Ceredo, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the morning of the 24th, putting the region squarely in a warm sector behind the boundary.  Abundant moisture and steeping low-level lapse rates were noted on the Pittsburgh morning sounding.  Storms were able to form in eastern Ohio ahead of a trough preceding a cold front by late afternoon. The line of thunderstorms pushed across portions of northern West Virginia and into southwest Pennsylvania into early evening, before mostly weakening as the storms passed northeast of Pittsburgh.  Abnormally high freezing levels helped to prevent much hail, but copious amounts of instability and shear allowed for numerous reports of wind damage, mostly to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that trees were blown down near Route 2 in New Martinsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved southward through the day of August 31st. Some instability was still able to develop near the boundary and a few showers and an isolated locally severe thunderstorm developed across portions of eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized (1.0 in diameter) hail was reported in Franklin.

A strengthening low pressure system and strong cold front crossed through West Virginia on the evening of the 24th, passing through a very warm and unstable environment. A line of severe thunderstorms stretched across Central Appalachia that afternoon and evening, resulting in widespread wind damage as well as two tornadoes touching down near the city of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst caused damagae near Wallace on the afternoon of the 24th, with estimated max wind speeds of 90-100 mph. The microburst began along Sissonville Drive just north of the intersection with Call Road. Limited tree damage was noted to the north and west of this point. More widespread tree damage was noted to the south along Wolf Pen Drive and Old Tuppers Creek Road. In addition to tree damage, a carport was destroyed and a fence was pushed over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) and its associated energy was moving across central West Virginia on the morning of August 20th. As this moved eastward into the region during the afternoon and evening hours, it served as the focus for additional shower and thunderstorm development. Significant amounts of instability and steepening low level lapse rates led some of these thunderstorms to become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down on Old Scherr Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system crossed the central Appalachians on the 2nd leading to generally light snow. A moderate to heavy band of snow developed as a piece of energy dug down on the back side of the upper low. This led to heavy snow across a fairly small area, southeastern Webster and Nicholas Counties. A social media report from near Parcoal, in Webster County, showed 7 inches of snow had fallen by late on the 2nd. A government official at Nettie, in Nicholas County, measured 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front, which has been lingering north of our area, marched south on July 6th and brought damaging storms and widespread flooding.   Sufficiently strong mid-level flow supported damaging wind in some cases. However, the main impacts were from flash flooding.  This was supported by the presence of anomalously high precipitable water values, deep warm cloud depths, and sufficient instability and lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 Call Center reported trees down at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rainfall across the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians on the 15th and 16th. Over a roughly 36 hour period, 1 to 2 inches of rain fell. This led to flooding along creeks, streams, and eventually smaller rivers as the water worked through the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding. Rocky Fork flooded, closing a section of Washington Street in Cross Lanes, and flooding along Venable Branch caused high water in the 3300 block of MacCorkle Avenue in Kanawha City. High water also closed portions of Campbells Creek Drive, Emma Way, Doc Bailey Road, and the 3000 block of East Dupont Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave trough and surface cold front brought scattered showers and thunderstorm on July 11th. Increasing shear, building instability, and mid-level dry resulted in a few strong storms across the area. Unseasonably high warm cloud depth created impressive rainfall rates, contributing to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local fire department reported a house surrounded by water and numerous streets flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundary moved into eastern Ohio on Tuesday, July 2nd. Temperatures rose into the upper-80s and dew points lingered in the upper-60s/low 70s. An increase in instability from a passing upper-level shortwave promoted the risk for thunderstorms. Mid-level dry air allowed updrafts to remain strong, thus damaging wind was a potential for this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 call center reported down trees along Oak Drive.

The high temperature at East Lynn Lake, a nearby Cooperative Observer station, was 90 degrees. Heat index values that afternoon topped out around 100 degrees.
