EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved southeastward from the Great Lakes and into the Mid-Atlantic states during the day of August 15th. At the surface, a boundary also moved southeastward from Ohio and into the region. With building instability, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the higher elevations in the mid afternoon hours, and then moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening. With relatively strong westerly to west-northwesterly flow aloft, deep layer shear increased and that, combined with the instability, led to some thunderstorms becoming strong to locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the 14400 block of South Fork Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure and its associated frontal boundaries crossed the region during the afternoon. A few of these storms grew in size enough to produce very large hail, with some hail stones as large as teacups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail stones ranged in diameter from one to three inches. The very large hail caused damage to roof coverings and vehicles. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak shortwaves in the mid-level flow lended support to thunderstorm development on August 20th.  Daytime heating was slowed by a cirrus shield earlier in the day, but later strong heating, continued moisture advection, and up to 30 knots of mid-level flow provided support for strong to severe thunderstorms.  Shear was weak overall, but the presence of ample downdraft CAPE supported a threat of wind damage from downbursts.  ||Storms forming in the mid to late afternoon hours, although becoming strong, did not produce any severe weather reports.  It was not until after 7 PM that storms were able to become strong enough to produce many reports of tree damage, with all of the WEst Virginia reports coming from a storm that crossed Marion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County 911 officials reported a number of trees down across Marion County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several ingredients were in place for scattered severe thunderstorms on August 8th.  An upper shortwave trough embedded in northwest flow crossed the Great Lakes during the day.  Moist west-southwest flow maintained dewpoints in the mid 60s, which in concert with daytime heating, fueled instability.  Strengthening mid-level wind contributed to sufficient levels of shear, allowing an organized line of thunderstorms to cross the region in advance of a cold front.  Steep low-level lapse rates promoted a damaging wind threat, but low-level shear was insufficient for a tornado threat.  The Storm Prediction Center place the region under a slight risk of severe weather for the day.||Thunderstorms began to form in earnest in southeast Ohio around 4 PM, creating wind damage in Marion and Preston Counties.  Numerous trees were blown down, and a hay shed was damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that trees were blown down and damage was done to a hay shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air picked up moisture from the Great Lakes and as it flowed over the mountains, this led to snow. Snowfall accumulations were around 3 to 6 inches for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.

Cold air picked up moisture from the Great Lakes and as it flowed over the mountains, this led to snow. Snowfall accumulations were around 3 to 6 inches for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.

A cold front crossed the region during the afternoon and evening hours at the same as an upper-level disturbance approached the region. A southerly flow out ahead of the boundary brought increased moisture and that combined with heating from sunshine led to unstable conditions by late in the day. Shear also increased late in the day in response to the approaching upper-level disturbance. This combination of instability, forcing, and wind shear led to showers and thunderstorms, one of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the 700 block of Mount Vista Trail. A couple of other trees were also blown down near the intersection of Mission Road and Mountainside Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm coverage in southern Ohio/Northern West Virginia was expected ahead of a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV). Temperatures were warm, mid-80s, and dewpoints lingered in the mid-60s to low-70s, and helped increase MLCAPE to 1000-1500 J/kg. A shortwave trough and increased mid-level flow traversed over the Ohio Valley the evening of May 26th, and helped spread forcing for ascent and increase shear over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local citizen reported hail to the size of 1 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture underneath a subsidence inversion resulted in areas of dense fog. Visibility was around one-quarter mile.

The risk for severe storms continued May 29th as necessary ingredients stayed in place. An unstable/ buoyant atmosphere, wind shear to sustain updrafts, and large scale |ascension with a crossing shortwave were all present. With a stalled boundary, and precipitable water values pushing up to 2 inches, flash flooding was also a concern throughout the entire day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported hail that varied in sizes. The largest was estimated to be half-dollar size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of increased orographic lift and weak perturbations within the southwest flow aloft, from an exiting upper-level ridge, helped break warm-air capping during the late morning on May 19th. Isolated and scattered storms developed along the Eastern Ridges.||A passing storm resulted in one man being killed and two others injured.  They were stricken by a falling tree while seeking shelter from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One man was killed and two were injured when a tree fell on them while on a kayaking trip near Seven Islands on the Cheat River. Story reported here: https://www.wvnews.com/prestoncountynews/news/kayaker-who-died-on-cheat-river-identified/article_23cdb16e-5aeb-5a46-90b5-6eedca78115c.html.

Low pressure passed by to the west on the 16th into the 17th. Ahead of the low, plenty of cold air remained in place, and this led to a period of snow on the morning of the 16th. Warmer air worked its way in aloft and this caused snow to change to rain with pockets of freezing rain later on the 16th into the morning of the 17th.

A couple mesoscale convective systems within a hot and humid air mass brought lines of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  The storms produced hail up to the size of quarters and produced damaging winds that blew down numerous trees and power lines.  The trees also damaged several structures.  In addition, the winds destroyed bleachers, damaged a chain link fence, and blew down a light pole at a little league baseball field in Bellepoint Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew siding and shingles off a house on Nubbins Ridge Road about two miles northwest of Spanishburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge was positioned over the East Coast May 18th, and pumped warm and moist air from the southwest. Skies were clear that morning and early afternoon, which supported day time mixing and steep low-level lapse rates. MUCAPE values neared 1500 J/kg in the evening and DCAPE values on the local 00Z sounding was over 1000 J/kg, indicating that storms had the capability to produce strong downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen reported hail up to 1 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passing of a prefrontal trough on the morning of May 23rd helped induce upper-level height falls and assisted with the strength of a frontal passage late in the day. There was plenty of moisture that evening, before the cold front passed, as dew points reached the upper-60s. Winds aloft became more northwest, allowing for a cool down of the upper-levels--increasing atmospheric instability. A strengthening of upper-level winds increased the threat of damaging winds, and the increase in shear created a scenario where convective updrafts can become stronger and long lived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 Call center reported a tree down near intersection of Route 50 and Route 92.

Low pressure tracked along a stalled boundary, passing through the area overnight on the 4th into the 5th. The lift associated with the low and the boundary combined with high amounts of moisture from the Atlantic Ocean and Gulf of Mexico, bringing heavy rain to the area. The rain led to several instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Henshaw Road was closed at Mill Creek due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passing over West Virginia caused rain showers that turned to snow as temperatures dropped, with accumulations reaching about one inch in spots around Mercer County.  With the colder temperatures, liquid water on the roads froze and created hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions caused a school bus to slide down a road and collide with a storage building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rainfall across the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians on the 15th and 16th. Over a roughly 36 hour period, 1 to 2 inches of rain fell. This led to flooding along creeks, streams, and eventually smaller rivers as the water worked through the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed due to flooding across the northern part of the county. Little Pond Run flooded, causing high water along Parkview Drive, College Parkway and Lakeview Drive. Core Road and Laurel Creek Road were closed due to flooding along Worthington Creek and Stillwell Creek flooded the 4900 block of Stillwell Road. Oak Grove Road was covered by about 4 feet of water from Big Run, and Carpenter Run and Sugarcamp Run flooded, covering Waverly Road in about 3 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of passing shortwaves produced rounds of accumulating snow showers across the region from roughly noon on December 4th to noon on the 5th.  The second shortwave was stronger, providing abundant moisture in the dendritic growth zone.  Also, northwest flow allowed for some enhancement off of Lake Erie.  Initial accumulation was hampered by a warm ground in many cases, especially away from the ridges.   A Winter Weather Advisory was issued for the ridges thanks to enhanced upslope and support from a strong mid-level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4.7 inches of snow was reported near White Oak Springs, and 3 inches of accumulation was seen at Eglon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed upper low over Ohio tracked northeast towards Canada and New England.  While near the Mid-Atlantic, this low created strong northwest flow over the Appalachian Mountains.  The upslope effect of the mountains utilized a moist air mass in the region to create snow showers in southeast WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow created upslope snow showers which created 6 inches of snow near Quinwood in under 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were closed due to flooding. This included Garretts Bend Road near the Elizabeth Baptist Church due to flooding along Hayzlett Fork and Trace Fork. Route 3 was closed near Duval Elementary School, and Fourmile Creek Road was closed near Kentuck Fork Road due to flooding along Fourmile Creek. Coal Branch flooded, closing a section of Coal Branch Road near Big Harts Road. Also, Ninemile Creek Road was closed near the Pine Grove Church due to flooding along Ninemile Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist advection and a shortwave crossing into the Ohio River Valley brought light precipitation to the region on the morning of December 13th.  Cold air in place at the surface, along with cooling southeasterly upslope flow, promoted a period of light freezing rain.  Although ice accretion totals were generally limited to a light glaze, the icing was enough to create a number of traffic incidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two known motor vehicle accidents occurred due to a light glaze of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial shortwave ahead of a trough over the central CONUS crossed the region on December 16th. Snow fell initially, with accumulations of 1 to 3 inches reported by the morning hours. However, a mix with freezing rain occurred as a warm layer developed aloft. Any icing in most cases was limited to a light glaze during the night of the 16th.||Locally heavy rainfall occurred through the night of the 16th into the morning of the 17th, thanks to the passage of a surface low and enhanced lift provided by an upper jet.  Rainfall totals of between 1 and 2 inches over a 24 hour period were common across northern West Virginia.  With the ground already saturated from previous precipitation, several flooding problems were able to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported that flooding caused a road closure near the intersection of Seven Pines Road and Buffalo Brink Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front lifted across the central Appalachians on the morning of the 13th. Temperatures aloft quickly rose above freezing, but surface temperatures lagged some as precipitation moved in. This created a period of freezing rain during the morning. Ice accretion amounts ranged from a trace to about a tenth of an inch across portions of central West Virginia. Light icing caused multiple accidents along I-79 in Braxton and Lewis Counties. The interstate was closed for several hours as crews worked to clean up the crashes and treat the road. One driver was taken to the hospital following one of the accidents in Braxton County. In Upshur County, there were 13 separate weather-related accidents.

Over a roughly 36 hour period, 1 to 2 inches of rain fell. This led to flooding along creeks, streams, and eventually smaller rivers as the water worked through the system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mud River flooded near Myra and Hamlin. The river gage along the Mud showed the river rose above its 10 foot flood stage on the evening of the 16th. It crested just over 13 feet on the morning of the 17th and returned to its banks a couple hours before sunrise on the 18th. This caused minor flooding of roads along the river, such as spots along Route 3 in West Hamlin and Peppermint Lane in Myra.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level low pressure crossed the region on December 2nd.  Increasing support for lift, as well as a favorable northwest flow, allowed numerous snow showers to impact the higher elevations of West Virginia, especially during the afternoon and evening hours. A few inches of snow were able to accumulate before snow rates decreased overnight as a result of thinning moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers reported 24 hour snow totals of 2.3 inches seven miles north of Thomas, and 3 inches near Terra Alta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist advection and a shortwave crossing into the Ohio River Valley brought light precipitation to the region on the morning of December 13th.  Cold air in place at the surface, along with cooling southeasterly upslope flow, promoted a period of light freezing rain.  Although ice accretion totals were generally limited to a light glaze, the icing was enough to create a number of traffic incidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three accidents were reported in Preston County as a result of a light glaze of freezing rain.  Two injuries occurred.

Low pressure intensified to the northeast and high pressure approached from the Midwest. A strong pressure gradient between these systems led to high winds across portions of eastern West Virginia. Wind gusts around 60 mph occurred.

A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes on the 26th and 27th. This pushed a cold front through the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians during the afternoon and evening of the 27th. Very strong synoptic winds behind the front led to thousands of power outages across the central part of West Virginia. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were measured. The highest measured gust was near Snowshoe in Pocahontas County where a mesonet station measured a 67 mph gust. Most damage was limited to trees and power lines, however a column at the front of the Westmoreland Baptist Church in Huntington was toppled by the wind.

With a strong pressure gradient across the region (due to a deepening low pressure system over the Great Lakes), deep low-level mixing, and jet dynamics, high winds were experienced across the region.  ||Wind gusts of 45-50 mph were measured across the region for sites below 2,000 ft. For sites in the ridges, 50-60 mph wind gusts were experienced. Isolated areas reported power lines and trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Site located 5 miles east of Canaan Heights.

A strong cold front passed across the central Appalachians and lower mid-Atlantic region on the morning and afternoon of the November 27th, supported aloft by a strong upper level disturbance.  The disturbance brought 55 to 65 MPH winds just off the surface, some of which funneled down to the surface.  Wind speeds decreased during the early morning hours of November 28th as the upper disturbance exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summers County 911 center reported numerous trees blown down by high winds at various locations across the northern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An onshore flow led to increasing amounts of moisture underneath a strong temperature inversion, and this led to the development of dense fog. Visibility was reported to be around one-quarter mile.

Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall occurred within a warm advection regime in advance of an approaching, highly amplified, mid-upper level trough. Rainfall totals of 1-2 inches were observed, leading to instances of flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Mill Creek at Bunker Hill exceeded the 4.25 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Henshaw Road was flooded under Interstate 81. The peak level of 4.26 feet occurred at 12:45 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall occurred within a warm advection regime in advance of an approaching, highly amplified, mid-upper level trough. Rainfall totals of 1-2 inches were observed, leading to instances of flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Opequon Creek at Martinsburg exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the indicated period, leading to flooding along portions of Douglas Grove Road (County Road 9/16) and Bowers Road. Additionally, water covered the stream access point at the Van Metre Ford Pedestrian Bridge. The peak level of 11.73 feet occurred at 5:00 PM.
